plainMeaning: Reverence to the awakened ones who began the path, who built the ford across the ocean of rebirth, and who awakened entirely on their own.

wordByWord.gloss: to the originators / beginning-makers (of the path and the teaching)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit āigara < Sanskrit ādikara: ādi ('beginning, origin') + kara ('maker, doer'); here in the dative–genitive plural ending -āṇaṁ ('to/of the …').
wordByWord.notes: Not in the preface glossary, so glossed in full: an āigara is one who sets the Dharma in motion afresh in an era. The ending -āṇaṁ carries the salutation 'to/for' implied from namotthuṇaṁ in the previous verse.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit titthayara < Sanskrit tīrthaṅkara: tīrtha ('ford, crossing-place') + kara ('maker'); dative–genitive plural -āṇaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term (tīrthaṅkara) — used, not re-defined. Here it names the founding of the four-fold saṅgha that ferries beings across saṁsāra.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sayaṁ ('self, by oneself') + saṁbuddha ('fully awakened', from saṁ- + √budh 'to awaken') < Sanskrit svayaṁsaṁbuddha; dative–genitive plural -āṇaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term (svayaṁsaṁbuddha) — used, not re-defined. Marks enlightenment attained without any external teacher.

literalTranslation: To the beginning-makers, to the ford-makers, to the self-awakened ones.
idiomaticTranslation: Reverence be to those who originate the path, who fashion the ford across the stream of rebirth, and who awaken wholly by their own power.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where the opening clause simply bowed to the Arihantas as 'bhagavanta' (worshipful), this second clause begins the substantive case for that reverence with a tightly logical triad. Read in reverse it discloses its own order. 'Sayaṁsaṁbuddha' is the ground: the Jina awakens with no teacher, decoding reality unaided — so whatever he gives, he first won alone. Because he is self-awakened he can be 'āigara', the one who re-originates the Dharma in an age where it had lapsed; he does not transmit a borrowed lamp but rekindles the flame. And because he originates it, he is 'titthayara', the ford-maker who shapes that rekindled teaching into a crossing — the four-fold saṅgha by which others, who are not self-awakened, may yet cross. The progression is thus from inner attainment (self-awakening) to public foundation (origination) to enduring institution (the ford). Notice too the rhythmic anaphora of the -āṇaṁ ending that binds the three titles into one breath of homage, a device the whole stava will exploit. This verse narrows the salutation from 'Arihanta' in general to the Tīrthaṅkara specifically — the rarest grade of Arihanta — and so sets up the cascade of 'best-of-men' titles that follows in the next clause.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The triad encodes a key Jain distinction: not every liberated soul is a Tīrthaṅkara. 'Sayaṁsaṁbuddha' separates the Tīrthaṅkara from the disciple who is awakened by another's instruction (parabodhita); the Tīrthaṅkara's bodhi is uninstructed. 'Āigara' affirms that Dharma is not invented but periodically re-revealed — beginningless truth given a fresh beginning in time. 'Titthayara' grounds the social fact of religion: liberation is preached and institutionalised as the four-fold tīrtha, the very crossing that gives the Tīrthaṅkara his name.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting these three words, a practitioner honours the chain that made their own practice possible: a being who needed no teacher nonetheless chose to found a teaching. It invites gratitude for the path itself and resolve to use the ford that was built — to cross, not merely to admire the bridge.

crossReferences.text: Namutthuṇaṁ (Praṇipāta-stava), clause 2, embedded in the Aupapātika (Uvavāiya) Sūtra

sources.title: Namotthunam Stotra (Śakra-stava) — text and explanation
sources.consultedFor: English rendering of the Namutthuṇaṁ clauses, including the 'originators of the path / self-enlightened' epithets of this verse.

sources.title: Tirtha, Dharma and Tirthankar (One Who Reveals the Dharma)
sources.consultedFor: Doctrinal sense of titthayara as founder of the four-fold tīrtha and the self-enlightened (sayaṁ-saṁbuddha) nature of a Tīrthaṅkara.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit roots underlying āigara (ādi + kara), tīrthaṅkara (tīrtha + kara), and saṁbuddha (saṁ- + √budh).

notes: The verse is an epithet triad, so commentary is kept tight per the length rule. The ending -āṇaṁ is dative–genitive plural; the governing 'reverence be to' is carried over from 'namotthuṇaṁ' in the preceding verse and is not repeated in the text itself. Book orthography preserved verbatim ('આઇગરાણં', 'સયંસંબુદ્ધાણં'). 'āigara' is sometimes rendered 'foremost/first-doer' rather than 'path-originator' in other editions; both reflect ādikara and are compatible. tīrthaṅkara and svayaṁsaṁbuddha are preface-glossary terms and are used without re-definition.
